result:
The first thing that comes is the smell of wet pavement after a summer storm, the way it rises like memory, metallic and sweet, carrying the ghost of every bicycle tire that ever hissed across it. I think of my grandmother’s porch in Mobile, 1994, where the wood was soft and splintered like the tongue of an old dog, and the azaleas bled fuchsia onto the brick. She kept a Maxwell House can full of bacon grease on the stove, a cloudy amber relic that could resurrect any wilted thing in a cast-iron pan. We sat shelling purple-hull peas into a colander, the beans ticking against the metal like light rain, while she told me hurricanes used to have only women’s names because “a man can’t keep up that much screaming for days.” I didn’t laugh then; I was seven and busy making a fleet of ant-sized boats from the pea pods, sailing them across the sweat-ringed table.

I carry that table with me the way some people carry piano keys in their fingers. It shows up in dreams where I’m always trying to finish dinner for guests who never arrive, chopping onions that turn to chalk, watching the stove clock melt backward. When I wake, the room smells faintly of scorched butter even though I haven’t cooked in days. My partner says I talk in my sleep about “keeping the fire small,” which sounds like a proverb I half remember but can’t source, like something stitched inside a hem to keep you humble.

There’s a theory that every childhood place stays occupied by a translucent duplicate of you, eternally the age you were then, still running the same circuits. If so, seven-year-old me is forever crouched behind that porch railing, breathing in mimic-grass and gumbo steam, convinced that if I can just listen hard enough I’ll decode the message the world is humming under its breath. I wonder whether she’s disappointed that thirty-eight-year-old me still hasn’t delivered it, or whether she’s relieved to remain the keeper of the secret, the one who never had to find out that the message is only “keep going.”

I keep going. I ride packed subway cars where strangers’ pulses travel through metal poles into my palms until I feel like a living circuit. I type sentences that start strong and finish limp, like origami cranes that unfolded mid-flight. I attend dinner parties where people speak of burnout the way medieval Christians once spoke of demons: an external possession, nothing that fair pay or four-day weeks couldn’t exorcise. I nod, pass the kale, think of lightning bugs trapped in mason jars, how their glow turns frantic when the oxygen thins.

Last week I found a Polaroid wedged behind my desk—my father holding me as an infant, both of us creased by the camera’s flash. He looks startled, like a man handed an intricate bomb he must keep from exploding long enough to pass it on. I never noticed before how his thumb presses against my ribs with the same pressure I now use when testing melons for ripeness. I emailed the photo to him without a subject line. He replied, “Funny how the older you get the more your parents come back to size.” I pictured him in the kitchen of the house I grew up in, now sold, the counter where I once lined up plastic dinosaurs now supporting someone else’s coffee maker. He was probably sipping tea from the mug I gave him at twelve—World’s #1 Dad in cracked cerulean. The dinosaurs are extinct again.

Entropy, my college physics professor insisted, is not disorder but the natural drift toward likelihood. A shuffled deck is not chaotic; it is simply exploring the nine-millionth permutation it forgot it already knew. I think of this when I watch crows hop along the roofline opposite my window, arranging and rearranging themselves like notes in a scale the sky keeps trying to hum. They remember faces, give gifts of bright buttons, hold funerals. Once I left out a peanut on the sill and came back to find it replaced by a blue barrette I’d lost in third grade. Impossible, of course, but there it was, its metal clasp still flecked with playground sand. I clipped it into my hair, felt the years accordion shut like a folding rule.

Tonight the city is a violin someone rosined too eagerly; every siren, every elevated train, every dog yelping at the moon slides along the same tensioned string. I walk until my shadow overtakes me, long and thin, a cartoon spilled by streetlight. In the park, a man sits on a bench reading a newspaper whose headlines are in a language I don’t recognize. He gestures for me to sit, folds a section into a paper boat, and launches it into the fountain where pennies gleam like sunken stars. We watch it absorb water, list, go under without a sound. He smiles as if we’ve confirmed an old wager, then returns to his incomprehensible news. I head home lighter, as though I’ve left a portion of my unfinished sentences swirling in that fountain, circling the drain with the wishes of strangers.

Back inside, my apartment smells of unmailed letters. I brew tea whose name translates to “monkey picked” though surely no primate hand plucked these withered leaves. The steam climbs the window, obscuring the crows now roosting like black commas punctuating the night. I turn on a single lamp, its glow a paperweight on the drifting room. Somewhere a neighbor laughs, the sound bright as a dropped glass. I open my notebook to a blank page, write “keep the fire small,” then cross it out, then write it again.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
